Ch1_HGLMM_Late season influence of beaver dams on surface water area

The purpose of this script is to explore the effect of beaver dams on surface water area, relative to local geomorphology, and contributing drainage area. This modeling focuses solely on late summer months; August & September.

Two models are presented herein. Both models have the same random effects structure for stream, year, and sample event. Model 1 examines the effect of beaver dam presence on surface water area, with no consideration for local context. Model 2 includes additional fixed effects of channel incision, riparian area, and drainage area.

Version summary:

Original iterations of these models had some issues that are addressed in this version.

1. Inappropriate hyperpriors for variance terms [sigma, sigma_stream, 
   sigma_year, etc.] constrained variance of posterior estimates to unrealistic 
   scales. This created the appearance of estimate precision, but was in fact
   modeling error.
   
   a. Original hyperprior for global residual variance in SWA; 
        
        sigma ~ Uniform(0,10) 
        
        - This makes no sense when the response variable is on a m^2 scale
        - This says that the variance in global SWA estimate can be anywhere
          between 0m^2 and 10m^2... WAY too constrained
   
2. In trying to address this issue ^ it became difficult to express my prior 
   expectations in terms of the raw SWA scale (m^2). So, I rescaled the response
   by
   
    - log-transforming SWA [to normalize the natural skew in SWA]
   
    - z-standardizing log(SWA)
   
   By rescaling SWA to z(log(SWA)), our hyperpriors are now in terms of standard
   deviations (b/c z-scaling). I think this is a more intuitive framework for 
   setting priors.
   
    b. Updated hyperprior for global residual variation in SWA
    
        sigma ~ Normal(0,1) Truncate(0,) 
        
        - This is a half-normal distribution with mean of 0, and precision 
          of 1.
        - This says that I expect the global residual variance (sigma) to be
          positive (which it has to be), and to be somewhere between 0 and 2
          (dnorm(0,1)).
          
        - For z(log(SWA)),the empirical SD is 1 because we z scaled it, so 
          this prior lines up with that scale; something I found difficult
          to do on the raw SWA scale
          
    c. ALL HYPERPRIORS HAVE BEEN UPDATED ACCORDINGLY

Data from USGS LBV Guston Coulee gage, near Saco, MT:

Late Summer Flow Conditions

2019:

Moderate August with peak in September

2021:

Low flow conditions throughout the summer season. Part of 3 year sustained drought conditions (2020, 2021, 2022).

Gage recorded no flow during August & September

2023:

Moderate to low August & September flows; after early season extreme flooding.

GEE-derived data for each stream:

Currently each incoming data frame is of the following structure:

Where:

- clusterID_seq = dammed/undammed sample pair ID
- window_type = whether a sample window is 'dammed' or 'undammed'
- watershed = watershed identifier (e.g., "BRZ" = Brazil Ck)
- year = year
- window_length = length (m) of stream flow line within sample window
- catchment_km2 = contributing catchment area/upstream drainage area (km^2)
- riparian_area_m2 = riparian area (m^2) of sample window; 2-m vertical
  buffer from DEM low point (stream flow line)
- TO = mean topographic openness across window
- p_window: avg dam detection prob for target window; see CP Ch1
- f_window: avg dam false positive prob for target window; see CP Ch1
- beav_count_all: all unique detected dams
- beav_count_conf: unique confidently detected dams
- anthro_count: all unique detected anthropogenic dams
- isAnthro: National Wetland Inventory (NWI) "dyked/impounded"
- Nhat = estimated dam count within sample window
  - ...= beav_count_all/(p_window(1-f_window)  [McCallum 2005]
- TO_mean = mean topographic openness across window
- time_period = 2wk interval 06/01 - 09/30
- SWA = surface water area (m^2) for window during time (t)
- t = time (t) {same as time_period}

—-

—-

Relative flow data.

NOTE: currently this represents data from the USGS Guston Coulee gage that has been z-standardized over the current data acquisition range (June-Sept; 2019, 2021, 2023).

Then, for each 2-week survey time period (1:8), the average relative flow \([mean(zFlow)]\) was computed, and added as a data column. IT IS ASSUMED that this flow data is representative of the RELATIVE flow pattern experienced throughout the study area during this time.

Add relFlow data:

Hierarchical Generalized Linear Mixed Effects Models

Proposed Models:

Both models:

\[ SWA \sim N(\mu, \sigma);\]

—-

M1:

\[\mu = \alpha + \eta_{stream} + \eta_{event} + \eta_{cluster} + (\beta1+\beta_{stream} + \beta_{year} + \beta_{event})*damPresence \]

—-

M2:

\[\mu = \alpha + \eta_{stream} + \eta_{event} + \eta_{cluster} + (\beta1+\beta_{stream} + \beta_{year} + \beta_{event})*damPresence + \beta_2 * Incision + \beta_3 * riparianArea + \beta_4 * drainageArea\]

—-

This section explores late summner SWA in relation to beaver dam presence.

Uses nested random slopes for beaver dam effect to allow for stream, year, 
and sampleEvent to cause deviation from the global mean beaver effect.

—-

Implement HGLMMs:

M3: Dam Presence Only; seasonal random slope (sampleEvent)

set.seed(12345)



Sys.time()

sink("HGLMM_damPres.jags")

cat("
model {

  for (i in 1:N) {
    SWA[i] ~ dnorm(mu[i], tau)

  mu[i] <- alpha
             + (beta1
                + b_stream[stream[i]] 
                + b_year[year[i]]
                + b_event[sampleEvent[i]]) * damPresence[i]
             + eta_stream[stream[i]]
             + eta_event[sampleEvent[i]]
             + eta_cluster[clusterID[i]]
  }

  # Fixed effects (standardized log-SWA scale)
  alpha ~ dnorm(0, 1)  #mean SWA (no dam) ~ 0 +- 1 sd
  beta1 ~ dnorm(0, 1)  #dam effect ~ 0 +- 1 sd

  # Random Effects
  # Stream/Watershed
  for (j in 1:n_stream) {
    eta_stream[j] ~ dnorm(0, tau_stream)     # random intercept for stream
    b_stream[j]   ~ dnorm(0, tau_b_stream)   # random slope for stream
  }
  
  # Year-level dam effect
  for(yr in 1:n_year) {
    b_year[yr] ~ dnorm(0, tau_b_year)        # random slope for year
  }
  
  # Event-level dam effect nested within year
  for (k in 1:n_event) {
    b_event[k] ~ dnorm(b_year[event_year[k]],tau_b_event)   # nested random slope
    eta_event[k] ~ dnorm(0, tau_event)       # random intercept for event
  }
  
  # Cluster ID
  for (c in 1:n_cluster) {
    eta_cluster[c] ~ dnorm(0, tau_cluster)    # random intercept for sampleCluster
  }

  # Hyperpriors (SDs on standardized log-SWA scale)
  sigma ~ dnorm(0, 1) T(0,)   # residual SD; half normal
  tau <- pow(sigma, -2)

  sigma_stream ~ dnorm(0,1) T(0,)
  tau_stream <- pow(sigma_stream, -2)

  sigma_b_stream ~ dnorm(0,0.7) T(0,)
  tau_b_stream <- pow(sigma_b_stream, -2)
  
  sigma_b_year ~ dnorm(0,0.7) T(0,)
  tau_b_year <- pow(sigma_b_year, -2)

  sigma_b_event ~ dnorm(0,0.7) T(0,)
  tau_b_event <- pow(sigma_b_event, -2)
  
  sigma_event ~ dnorm(0,1) T(0,)
  tau_event <- pow(sigma_event, -2)
  
  sigma_cluster ~ dnorm(0,1) T(0,)
  tau_cluster <- pow(sigma_cluster, -2)
}
", fill = TRUE)

sink()

M3: Prior-Posterior Overlap:

Check global intercept and global beaver effect slope

PRIORS FROM M3:
alpha  ~ dnorm(0, 1)
beta1  ~ dnorm(0, 1)

M3 posterior mean dam effect [mean(beta1)]:

## [1] 0.09405269

This is on a z-standardized log(SWA) scale ^

—-

Compute predicted dam effects and baseline SWA conditions:

Convert from z(log(SWA)) back to SWA (m^2):

Compute “typical dam effect across watersheds and late summer sampling events”

Mean predicted dam effect for ‘typical’ stream/year/event: +- 95% CI

(m^2)

## [1] 250.9727
##      2.5% 
## -588.3746
##    97.5% 
## 2230.548

On the raw SWA scale (m^2).

This says that dammed reaches, for a typical stream/year/event, have 243.43 m^2 more SWA than un-dammed reaches… with LOTS of variation.

Compute global proportional change in SWA caused by dam presence for typical stream/year/event: +- 95% CI

## [1] "M3 Average proportional change from baseline caused by dam presence:"
## [1] "+- 95% CI"
## [1] 1.297328
##       2.5% 
## -0.6701184
##    97.5% 
## 7.426897

Heatmap of proportional change in SWA across watersheds and sampling events

1.28 =~ 128% increase from baseline conditions

with LOTS of variation; in some cases, dammed reaches have less water than undammed. in other cases, dammed reaches have LOTS more water than un-dammed.

—-

Compare b1 trajectory across event, by stream:

Interestingly, Brazil sees the highest proportional difference, though this is likely confounded by geomorphic conditions, and will likely change in M4.

For the most part, though, dams do seem to increase late summer surface water area to some degree, when compared to un-dammed reaches.

NOTE

Structural changes between m3 and m4 shift parameter interpretations, and caution should be used in comparison.

The M3 plots presented above represent the model-estimated global dam effect, with geomorphology and drainage area effects implicitly included by the random effects structure. Thus, \(\alpha\) is the baseline SWA with 0 dam, across all geomorphic/drainage conditions in the dataset (i.e., unconditional on geomorphology). AS a result, the ‘baseline’ conditions \((\alpha)\), and dam effects \(\beta1\) are inflated because the intercept and random effects parameters are forced to account for all of the variance explained by covariates in M4.

By including z-std covariates in M4, \(\alpha\) becomes “baseline SWA with 0 dam, at mean geomorphic/drainage conditions”. Further, in M4, \(\beta1\) becomes the global average dam effect at mean geomorphic/drainage conditions; \([\beta_{stream}, \beta_{year}, \beta_{event}]\) represent stream/year/event deviations from that global dam effect (at mean covariate conditions).

M4: Dam Presence + Context with updated random effects structure

set.seed(12345)

Sys.time()

sink("HGLMM_allCovar.jags")

cat("
    model {

  for (i in 1:N) {
    
  SWA[i] ~ dnorm(mu[i], tau)

  mu[i] <- alpha
             + (beta1
                + b_stream[stream[i]] 
                + b_year[year[i]]
                + b_event[sampleEvent[i]]) * damPresence[i]
             + beta2 * TO[i] 
             + beta3 * RIPA[i]
             + beta4 * UDA[i]
             + eta_stream[stream[i]]
             + eta_event[sampleEvent[i]]
             + eta_cluster[clusterID[i]]
  }

  # Fixed effects
  alpha ~ dnorm(0, 1)
  beta1 ~ dnorm(0, 1)
  beta2 ~ dnorm(0, 1)
  beta3 ~ dnorm(0, 1)
  beta4 ~ dnorm(0, 1)

  # Random Effects
  # Stream/Watershed
  for (j in 1:n_stream) {
    eta_stream[j] ~ dnorm(0, tau_stream)     # random intercept for stream
    b_stream[j]   ~ dnorm(0, tau_b_stream)   # random slope for stream
  }
  
  # Year-level dam effect
  for(yr in 1:n_year) {
    b_year[yr] ~ dnorm(0, tau_b_year)        # random slope for year
  }
  
  # Event-level dam effect nested within year
  for (k in 1:n_event) {
    b_event[k] ~ dnorm(b_year[event_year[k]],tau_b_event)       # nested random slope
    eta_event[k] ~ dnorm(0, tau_event)
  }
  
  # Cluster ID
  for (c in 1:n_cluster) {
    eta_cluster[c] ~ dnorm(0, tau_cluster)
  }

  # Hyperpriors
  sigma ~ dnorm(0, 1) T(0,)   # half-normal with sd ≈ sd_SWA
  tau <- pow(sigma, -2)

  sigma_stream ~ dnorm(0, 1) T(0,)
  tau_stream <- pow(sigma_stream, -2)

  sigma_b_stream ~ dnorm(0, 0.7) T(0,)
  tau_b_stream <- pow(sigma_b_stream, -2)
  
  sigma_b_year ~ dnorm(0, 0.7) T(0,)
  tau_b_year <- pow(sigma_b_year, -2)

  sigma_b_event ~ dnorm(0, 0.7) T(0,)
  tau_b_event <- pow(sigma_b_event, -2)
  
  sigma_event ~ dnorm(0, 1) T(0,)
  tau_event <- pow(sigma_event, -2)
  
  sigma_cluster ~ dnorm(0, 1) T(0,)
  tau_cluster <- pow(sigma_cluster, -2)
}
", fill = TRUE)

sink()

—-

NOTE:

TO was transformed to “channel incision” by taking the inverse of proportion openness:

\[ChannelIncision = 1 - (TO/90) \]

Where \((TO/90)\) is the proportion of max openness (90 degrees = “flat”/“fully open”)

—-

m4 Mean Dam Effect:

## [1] 0.07621684

m4 Mean TO Effect

Interpretation: z(log(SWA)) change per 1 standard deviation increase in channel incision

## [1] -0.3649625

Interpretation: z(log(SWA)) change per 1 standard deviation increase in riparian area

## [1] 0.02520952

m4 Mean CDA Effect

Interpretation: z(log(SWA)) change per 1 standard deviation increase in contributing drainage area

## [1] 0.5228496

Compute “typical dam effect across watersheds and late summer sampling events”

Mean predicted dam effect for ‘typical’ stream/year/event: +- 95% CI

(m^2)

## [1] 63.03895
##      2.5% 
## -61.60142
##    97.5% 
## 616.7948

On the raw SWA scale (m^2).

When you control for site-level differences in geomorphology and contributing drainage area, dammed reaches, for a typical stream/year/event, have ~63 m^2 more SWA than un-dammed reaches. Still lots of variation, though more of it is explained by geomorphology/drainage area in M4.

—-

Estimated baseline conditions:

## [1] 91.01868

baseline = 92 m^2 on average

Compute global proportional change in SWA caused by dam presence for typical stream/year/event:

+- 95% CI

## [1] 0.6385323
##       2.5% 
## -0.6420333
##    97.5% 
## 4.370283

Heatmap of proportional change in SWA across watersheds and sampling events

0.63 =~ 63% increase from baseline conditions

With LOTS of variation; in some cases, dammed reaches have less water than undammed. in other cases, dammed reaches have LOTS more water than un-dammed. Again, though, the scale has shifted, because more of the immense variation in SWA was explained by geomorph./drainage area.

Compare b1 trajectory across event, by stream:

Similar patterns to M3, though a few positional changes among watersheds. NOTE as well, the scale change from M3 (0,8) to M4 (0, 4.5).
